perturbation.original_text: Reseller shall: (a) not do or omit to do, nor authorize any third party to do or omit to do, any act or omission that would or might invalidate or be inconsistent with any of Supplier's intellectual property rights; (b) promptly notify Supplier of any actual, threatened, or suspected infringement of any of Supplier's intellectual property rights that comes to Reseller's notice; and (c) at Supplier's request and expense, do all such things as may be reasonably required to assist Supplier in taking or resisting any proceedings in relation to any such infringement or claim.
perturbation.changed_text: Reseller shall: (a) endeavor not to do or omit to do, nor authorize any third party to do or omit to do, any act or omission that would or might invalidate or be inconsistent with any of Supplier's intellectual property rights; (b) notify Supplier of any actual infringement of any of Supplier's intellectual property rights that comes to Reseller's confirmed knowledge; and (c) at Supplier's request and expense, do such things as Supplier deems appropriate to assist Supplier in taking or resisting any proceedings in relation to any such infringement or claim.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'not do or omit to do' to 'endeavor not to do or omit to do,' 'promptly notify' to 'notify...of any actual infringement...that comes to Reseller's confirmed knowledge,' and 'all such things as may be reasonably required' to 'such things as Supplier deems appropriate,' the obligations become less stringent and more subjective. 'Endeavor' creates a lower standard of effort than a strict prohibition. Requiring 'confirmed knowledge' before notification introduces a higher burden of proof for the supplier, and allowing the Supplier to define appropriate action grants discretion that may conflict with the need for urgent and comprehensive action required to protect intellectual property rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act places a responsibility on parties to prevent trademark infringement. The original text had clearer obligations to protect the Supplier's IP. The modified text, by using 'endeavor,' 'confirmed knowledge,' and 'Supplier deems appropriate,' weakens the Reseller's responsibility to actively prevent infringement and protect the Supplier's trademark, making it potentially non-compliant with the intent of the Lanham Act, which requires active measures to protect trademark rights.

Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant- (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any g
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that weaken the Reseller's obligations to protect the Supplier's intellectual property, potentially contradicting the intent of the Lanham Act. The original text used definitive language like 'not do or omit to do' and 'promptly notify,' creating clear responsibilities. The changed text uses softer terms like 'endeavor not to do or omit to do' and 'notify...of any actual infringement...that comes to Reseller's confirmed knowledge,' which reduces the Reseller's accountability. This shift towards more subjective and less stringent obligations could be seen as undermining the active measures required to protect trademark rights, as the Lanham Act generally aims to prevent trademark infringement. Although the scraped snippet (15 USC 1114) focuses on remedies for infringement rather than preventative measures, the broader context of trademark law implies a responsibility to prevent infringement, which the changes in the contract weaken.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall: (i) safeguard and keep secret all Confidential Information, and will not directly or indirectly disclose to any third party the Confidential Information without written permission of the other; and (ii) in performing its obligations hereunder, use at least the same degree of care as it does with respect to its own confidential information of like importance but, in any event, at least reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party should: (i) take steps to safeguard Confidential Information, and will consider not directly or indirectly disclosing the Confidential Information to any third party; and (ii) in performing its obligations hereunder, use care when handling confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall safeguard and keep secret' to 'take steps to safeguard', 'will not disclose' to 'will consider not disclosing', and 'use at least the same degree of care as it does with respect to its own confidential information of like importance but, in any event, at least reasonable care' to 'use care when handling confidential information' significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The new language is vague and subjective, making it difficult to enforce and potentially allowing for negligent handling of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York Trade Secret Law requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The modified text replaces clear obligations to 'safeguard and keep secret' with vague suggestions to 'take steps to safeguard,' which may not meet the legal standard of reasonable measures, potentially failing to adequately protect trade secrets as required by New York law. Specifically, 'use care when handling confidential information' is less descriptive than 'use at least the same degree of care as it does with respect to its own confidential information of like importance but, in any event, at least reasonable care,' leaving room for interpretation and possible negligence.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates specific actions to protect confidential information, whereas the modified version weakens these obligations by using vague and discretionary language. The original text says each party 'shall safeguard and keep secret' and 'will not disclose,' while the modified text says each party 'should take steps to safeguard' and 'will consider not disclosing'. This change introduces ambiguity and reduces the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement. The LLM Law Explanation specifically mentions that New York Trade Secret Law requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, and that the modified text might not meet that standard. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly mention trade secret law, the general idea of an obligation being weakened to the point of being unenforceable is what creates the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York and all disputes and controversies arising out of or in connection with the Agreement shall be brought exclusively before the competent courts in New York County, New York; provided however that judgment shall be enforceable in any country and that nothing in this Section shall prevent or restrict either Party from seeking interim relief in any competent jurisdiction as it may deem fit.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted under the general principles of law, and disputes may be brought before a mutually agreed upon forum; provided however that judgment enforcement shall be subject to international conventions, and either Party may seek remedies as it sees appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York' to 'shall be interpreted under the general principles of law' removes the specific jurisdiction, making the applicable law uncertain. Replacing 'brought exclusively before the competent courts in New York County, New York' with 'may be brought before a mutually agreed upon forum' makes the dispute resolution process ambiguous and dependent on future agreement. Changing 'either Party from seeking interim relief in any competent jurisdiction as it may deem fit' to 'either Party may seek remedies as it sees appropriate' broadens the possible range of permissible relief to the point of incomprehensibility. The modified text provides no clear framework for interpreting or enforcing the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) - Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-301 allows parties to choose the governing law of their contract, provided there is a reasonable relation to the chosen state. By removing the specific choice of New York law and specifying 'general principles of law' instead, the modified text avoids making an express choice of law that New York's UCC honors. This creates uncertainty and could lead to disputes over which jurisdiction's laws apply, undermining the purpose of the UCC provision, which aims to provide clarity and predictability in commercial transactions. Additionally, by stating disputes 'may' be brought before a mutually agreed upon forum leaves the dispute resolution process open, possibly conflicting with established court jurisdictions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), [the Uniform Commercial Code] applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of [the Uniform Commercial Code] specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402 ; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106 ; (3) Section 4-102 ; (4) Section 4A-507 ; (5) Section 5-116 ; [(6) Section 6-103 ; (7) Section 8-110 ; (8) Sections 9-301 through 9 - 307 . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts UCC § 1-301, which allows parties to choose the governing law of their contract if the transaction bears a reasonable relation to the chosen state, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: "when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties." By replacing the specific choice of New York law with 'general principles of law,' the changed text fails to make an express choice of law. Additionally, the phrase 'disputes may be brought before a mutually agreed upon forum' introduces ambiguity into the dispute resolution process, potentially conflicting with established court jurisdictions.
